zh.lifeSummary: Sir Simon III Fraser, The Patriot Laird of Oliver Castle and Neidpath Castle於1226年出生。他於1265年之前與Lady Mary Bisset of Oliver Castle結婚。他們至少是3個兒子和2個女兒的父母。他於1266年住在美國新澤西州薩萊姆洛維爾 阿洛維艾斯 可可 堂史普艾其培利其可 易曼德。他去世於1306年在英國倫敦享年80歲，埋葬在英國英格蘭倫敦。

zh-hans.lifeSummary: Sir Simon III Fraser, The Patriot Laird of Oliver Castle and Neidpath Castle于1226年出生。他于1265年之前与Lady Mary Bisset of Oliver Castle结婚。他们至少是3个儿子和2个女儿的父母。他于1266年住在美国新泽西州薩萊姆洛維爾 阿洛維艾斯 可可 堂史普艾其培利其可 易曼德。他去世于1306年在英国倫敦享年80岁，埋葬在英国英格兰倫敦。

nameMeanings.name: Fraser,
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Scottish: apparently a nickname from Anglo-Norman French fraser(e), fresere ‘strawberry plant’. Fresel and Frisel, from Anglo-Norman French fresel ‘strawberry’, are early variants of the Fraser surname, the modern Gaelicized form of which is Friseal. See Frizzell . The crest on the Scottish family's coat of arms takes the form of a strawberry plant, but its antiquity is unknown. Claims of a habitational derivation, in particular from a place called la Frézelière in Anjou (France), are attractive but they lack verifiable evidence.
Americanized form of one or more similar (like-sounding) Jewish surnames.

History: There are two clans Fraser in Scotland, with common ancestry going back to Simon Fraser of Keith in East Lothian, who lived in the 12th century. One of these clans has its seat at Philorth Castle (subsequently re-named Cairnbulg) on the northeast coast of Scotland. Sir Alexander Fraser, 8th laird of Philorth (c. 1536–1623) converted the fishing village of Faithlie into the burgh of Fraserburgh in the 1590s. The other clan is Fraser of Lovat, associated mainly with the city of Inverness are. They are descended from Simon Fraser, a younger son, who lived in the early 14th century. In Gaelic, the head of Clan Fraser of Lovat is known as Mac Shimi ‘son of Simon’.

variants.name: Frayser
meanings: Probably an altered form of   Fraser  .

variants.name: Frasier
meanings: Scottish and northern Irish: variant of   Fraser  .

variants.name: Frasure
meanings: Altered form of Scottish   Fraser  .

variants.name: Frazer
meanings: Variant of   Fraser  , associated chiefly with northern Ireland.

variants.name: Fraizer
meanings: Altered form of Scottish   Fraser  .

variants.name: Frashier
meanings: Scottish and northern Irish: variant of   Fraser  .

variants.name: Fraiser
meanings: Altered form of Scottish   Fraser  .

variants.name: Frizzell
meanings: English and Irish: nickname from Old French fresel, frisel ‘lace, ribbon, braid, brooch, frill, ruff’, for someone with an ornate style of dress, or ‘object of little value’ (a diminutive of frese, of ancient Germanic origin).
Scottish: from Anglo-Norman French fresel ‘strawberry’, an alternative form of Fraser .

variants.name: Frazier
meanings: English and Scottish: variant of   Fraser  .
Americanized form of French Frégeau (see Fregeau ).

variants.name: Frasher
meanings: Scottish and northern Irish: variant of   Fraser  .
Possibly an altered form of German Fratzscher (see Frutchey ).

nameMeanings.name: The
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Vietnamese: unexplained.
Chinese: variant Romanization of the surname 鄭, possibly based on its Hokkien or Teochew pronunciation, see   Zheng   1.

variants.name: Zheng
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese Jian, Yi, Bin, Hong, Hua, Li, Wei, Yong, Ting, Xin, Yan, Hui, Min, Hao, Shen, Da, Quan, Tian, Chong.
Chinese:

Mandarin form of the surname 鄭: from Zheng (鄭), the name of a state (located in part of present-day Shaanxi and Henan provinces) granted to You, the younger brother of King Xuan of Zhou (reigned 827–782  BC ). The state of Zheng was annexed by the state of Han (located in present-day Shaanxi and Henan provinces) during the Warring States period (475–221  BC ).

nameMeanings.name: Laird
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Scottish and northern Irish: status name for a landlord or landowner, from northern Middle English laverd ‘lord’.

variants.name: Lard
meanings: Scottish: altered form of   Laird  .

variants.name: Leard
meanings: Scottish and northern Irish: variant of   Laird  .

nameMeanings.name: of
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: German: possibly a metonymic occupational name for a fisherman, from orfe, a species of freshwater fish. Alternatively, a habitational name from Urff near Kassel, formerly Orpha.

variants.name: Orff
meanings: German: variant of   Orf  .

nameMeanings.name: Oliver
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (northern), southern Scottish, southern French, and German: from the Old French personal name Oliver (modern French Olivier). This became common largely through the influence of the immensely popular narrative poem Chanson de Roland, in which the warrior Oliver is the wise best friend of the doomed hero Roland. The two men were peers at the court of Charlemagne, and the name is probably of ancient Germanic origin. The name ostensibly means ‘olive tree’ (see Oliveira ), but this is almost certainly the result of folk etymology working on an unidentified ancient Germanic personal name, perhaps a cognate of Alvaro . Old Norse Óláf (see Olliff ) and ancient Germanic Alfhari (composed of the elements alf ‘elf’ and hari ‘army’) have also been suggested as sources, but both personal names are difficult to explain phonetically, especially the latter. The Anglo-Norman name has been established in Ireland (Louth) since at least the 14th century, and was reinforced in Ulster and Limerick by migrants from England in the 17th century. The surname is also borne by Jews, apparently as an adoption of the non-Jewish surname.
Catalan: generally a topographic name from oliver ‘olive tree’, but in some instances possibly related to a homonymous personal name (see 1 above).
History: The surname Oliver of French origin (see 1 above) is listed in the register of Huguenot ancestors recognized by the Huguenot Society of South Carolina.

variants.name: Oliveros
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jose, Francisco, Pedro, Alfredo, Gilberto, Juan, Arturo, Carlos, Javier, Jesus, Jorge, Luis.
Spanish: from an old variant of the personal name Olivero (see Oliver ).

variants.name: Oliveira
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Manuel, Jose, Carlos, Luis, Fernando, Mario, Luiz, Francisco, Sergio, Ana, Geraldo, Pedro.  Portuguese Joao, Paulo, Joaquim, Duarte, Agostinho, Guilherme, Marcio, Sebastiao, Anabela, Henrique, Messias, Ademir.

Portuguese and Galician: habitational name from any of numerous places called Oliveira, from Portuguese and Galician oliveira ‘olive grove’ (from Late Latin olivarius, a derivative of Latin oliva ‘olive’), or a topographic name from this word. Compare De Oliveira .

Jewish (Sephardic): adoption of the Iberian surname (see 1 above) at the moment of conversion to Roman Catholicism. After the return to Judaism (generations later), some descendants retained the name their families used as Catholics.

variants.name: Oliverson
meanings: Scottish: patronymic from   Oliver  .

variants.name: Oliviero
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Antonio, Amedeo, Aniello, Ciro, Dante, Gennaro, Pasquale, Pietro, Rocco, Salvatore, Silvio, Ugo.  Spanish Mario, Ademar, Guadalupe, Jose, Ricardo.
Italian: from the personal name Oliviero, from the Old French personal name Olivier (see Oliver ).

variants.name: Olivier
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Pierre, Jacques, Michel, Andre, Antoine, Julien, Marcel, Armand, Benoit, Edouard, Emile, Henri.
French: from the personal name Olivier, standard French form of Oliver .

variants.name: Olver
meanings: English (Cornwall and Devon): variant of   Oliver  .

variants.name: Olivero
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Luis, Jose, Mercedes, Ana, Andres, Armando, Cesar, Domitila, Dulce, Feliciano, Fernanda, Fernando.  Italian Antonio, Aldo, Carlo, Carmela, Carmelo, Franco, Gabriele, Marco.
Spanish and Italian: from the personal name Olivero (see Oliver ).
Italian: from Old Italian olivero, which, like olivaro, was used to denote an olive tree, and may also have been applied as a nickname for someone who owned or cultivated olive trees.

nameMeanings.name: Castle
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: topographic name from Anglo-Norman French, Middle English castel ‘castle, fortified building or set of buildings’, especially the residence of a feudal lord (from Late Latin castellum, a diminutive of castrum ‘fort, Roman walled city’). The name would also have denoted a servant who lived and worked at such a place, or someone who had an obligation to provide services or rent to a castle.

variants.name: Castell
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Roberto, Amarilys, Carlos, Eduardo, Estevan, Francisco, Guadalupe, Humberto, Jorge, Manuel, Mateo, Pablo.
English and Scottish: variant of   Castle  .

Manx: shortened form of Gaelic Mac Asgaill ‘son of Ásketill’, an Old Norse personal name composed of the elements áss ‘god’ + ketill ‘sacrificial cauldron, helmet’. The Manx surname is cognate with Scottish Gaelic McAskill .

variants.name: Cassell
meanings: English and Irish: habitational name of Norman origin, from Cassel in the Nord department, Flanders, France.
English: sometimes a variant of   Castle  .
Irish: shortened Anglicized form of Ó Caisile, which is probably in origin a variant of Ó Caiside, see Cassidy .

variants.name: Castel
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Benoit, Herve, Jean Michel, Thierry, Yves.  Spanish Jose, Blanco, Carlos, Rafael.
English: variant of   Castle  .
Aragonese, Catalan, and Jewish (Sephardic): variant of   Castell   3, a cognate of 5 below.

variants.name: Castles
meanings: English: variant of Castle , with post-medieval excrescent -s.
Irish (Armagh and Meath): in Ireland, this may be the English name, but it may also be an Anglicized form of Ó Caisil; see Cashell .

nameMeanings.name: and
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Jewish Hyman, Isidor, Myer.

English, German, and Jewish (Ashkenazic): metonymic occupational name for a maker of hoops and bands, etc., from Middle English band, bond, Middle High German, Middle Low German bant, German Band ‘hoop, metal band, fetter, shackle’, i.e. something used for tying or binding.
English: variant of   Bond  .

variants.name: Bandt
meanings: German: variant of   Band  .

variants.name: Bandstra
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Dutch Gerrit, Wouter.
West Frisian: habitational name composed with Band 4 + the suffix -stra from Old Frisian sittera ‘inhabitant of’.

variants.name: Banta
meanings: West Frisian: probably a habitational name for someone from Bant, in the 17th century an island in Friesland, now a village north of Emmeloord in the Noordoostpolder (see   Band   4, compare   Banda   5). This surname is no longer found in the Netherlands.
History: Epke Jacobs Banta arrived in New Netherland in 1659.

variants.name: Bandel
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Jewish Zvi, Emanuel, Hyman, Meir.
South German and Jewish (Ashkenazic): from a diminutive of   Band  . See also   Bendel  .
German: in some cases, a variant of Pandel, a pet form of the medieval personal name Pantaleon (see Pantaleo ).

variants.name: Bend
meanings: English (Leicestershire and Nottinghamshire): from Middle English bend ‘band, fetter, chain; ribbon, sash, stripe’, for someone who made, wore, or used such items. See also the Band 1 and compare Bendy 1.

variants.name: Bandler
meanings: German: occupational name for a maker of band (for vessels) or ribbon or lace, from an agent derivative of Middle High German bandel, a diminutive of bant ‘band’ (see Band ).

variants.name: Bandy
meanings: English: perhaps from an unrecorded Middle English bandi, bondi, bendi, derivatives of Middle English and Old English band, bond, bend ‘band, ribbon, stripe’ + Middle English -i, reinforced by Old French bendé ‘striped’, with reference perhaps to clothing ornamented with ribbons or stripes, or to the diagonal band of a heraldic device. Compare Band .
English: alternatively, perhaps from an unrecorded Middle English bandy, bendy ‘curved stick’, used for someone with bent or crooked legs.
Czech and Slovak: from a derivative of banda ‘(gipsy) band’, apparently a nickname for a bandsman. This surname is found among Gypsies.

variants.name: Bond
meanings: English: status name for a peasant farmer or husbandman, Middle English bond(e), bounde, occasionally bande ‘bondman, customary tenant, serf’ (Old English bonda, bunda, reinforced by Old Norse bóndi). The Old Norse word was also in use as a personal name (Old Norse Bóndi, Bondi, Bundi, Bonde, borrowed as late Old English Bonda), and this has given rise to other English and Scandinavian surnames alongside those originating as status names, such as the Middle English personal name Bonde. The status of the peasant farmer fluctuated considerably during the Middle Ages; moreover, the underlying ancient Germanic word is of disputed origin and meaning. Among ancient Germanic peoples who settled to an agricultural life, the term came to signify a farmer holding lands from, and bound by loyalty to, a lord; from this developed the sense of a free landholder as opposed to a serf. In England after the Norman Conquest the word sank in status and became associated with the notion of bound servitude. The name can also be a variant of Band .
Swedish: variant of   Bonde  .
In some cases also an American shortened form of Ukrainian Bondarenko and possibly also of some other surname beginning with Bond-.

nameMeanings.name: Castle
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: topographic name from Anglo-Norman French, Middle English castel ‘castle, fortified building or set of buildings’, especially the residence of a feudal lord (from Late Latin castellum, a diminutive of castrum ‘fort, Roman walled city’). The name would also have denoted a servant who lived and worked at such a place, or someone who had an obligation to provide services or rent to a castle.

Irish (Armagh and Meath): in Ireland, this may be the English name, but it may also be an Anglicized form of Ó Caisil; see Cashell .

nameMeanings.name: Simon
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Usual English form of Simeon , borne in the New Testament by various characters: two apostles, a brother of Jesus, a Pharisee, a leper, a tanner, a sorcerer (who offered money for the gifts of the Holy Ghost, giving rise to the term simony), and the man who carried Jesus's cross to the Crucifixion.

variants.name: Simen
meanings: Usual English form of Simeon , borne in the New Testament by various characters: two apostles, a brother of Jesus, a Pharisee, a leper, a tanner, a sorcerer (who offered money for the gifts of the Holy Ghost, giving rise to the term simony), and the man who carried Jesus's cross to the Crucifixion.

nameMeanings.name: III
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Japanese Yuko, Aiko, Hideaki, Hiroki, Hiroyuki, Isamu, Kaoru, Katsumi, Keiichiro, Kenji, Masako, Masayuki.
Japanese: variously written, sometimes with characters used phonetically; the most probable meanings are 仁井 ‘benevolent well’, 新居 ‘new residence’, or 新井 ‘new well’. It is not common in Japan.

historicEvents.title: 100 Years of Conflict Between England and France Ends
historicEvents.description: Treaty of Paris was signed on December 4, 1259, which ended 100 years of conflict between England and France.

historicEvents.title: Jews Expelled From England
historicEvents.description: After years of anti-Semitic behavior, King Edward I expelled Jews from England in 1290.

en.lifeSummary: Sir Simon III Fraser, The Patriot Laird of Oliver Castle and Neidpath Castle was born in 1226. He married Lady Mary Bisset of Oliver Castle before 1265. They were the parents of at least 3 sons and 2 daughters. He lived in EDGEPELLICK ISLAND, Lower Alloways Creek Township, Salem, New Jersey, United States in 1266. He died in 1306, in London, England, at the age of 80, and was buried in London, England, United Kingdom.

fr.lifeSummary: Sir Simon III Fraser, The Patriot Laird of Oliver Castle and Neidpath Castle est né en 1226. Il a épousé Lady Mary Bisset of Oliver Castle avant 1265. Ils ont eu au moins 3 fils et 2 filles. Il a vécu à EDGEPELLICK ISLAND, Lower Alloways Creek Township, Salem, New Jersey, États-Unis en 1266. Il est décédé en 1306, à Londres, Angleterre, à l’âge de 80 ans, et a été enterré à Londres, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni.

Morì nel 1306, nel London, England, all'età di 80 anni, e fu sepolto a London, Inghilterra, Regno Unito.

ja.lifeSummary: Sir Simon III Fraser, The Patriot Laird of Oliver Castle and Neidpath Castleは1226年に生まれた。1265年以前にLady Mary Bisset of Oliver Castleと結婚した。彼らは少なくとも3人の息子と2人の娘の親であった。彼は1266年にはアメリカ合衆国ニュージャージー州サレムロウエル アロワイズ クレエク トウンシュイップエジェペリック イスランドに住んでいた。彼は1306年にイギリスロンドンで80歳で死去し，イギリスイギリスロンドンにて埋葬された。

ko.Brotherton Scotland: 미국 뉴저지 주 살렘 로어 앨로아이스 크리크 타운시프 에지프릭 이슬랜드
ko.lifeSummary: Sir Simon III Fraser, The Patriot Laird of Oliver Castle and Neidpath Castle(은)는 1226년 태어났다. 그는 1265년 이전 Lady Mary Bisset of Oliver Castle(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 세 명의 아들과 두 명의 딸을 두었다. 1266년도에 미국 뉴저지 주 살렘 로어 앨로아이스 크리크 타운시프 에지프릭 이슬랜드에서 살았다. 그는 1306년 영국 런던에서 80세의 나이로 사망했으며 영국 잉글랜드 런던에 묻혔다.

pt.lifeSummary: Sir Simon III Fraser, The Patriot Laird of Oliver Castle and Neidpath Castle nasceu em 1226. Ele casou-se com Lady Mary Bisset of Oliver Castle antes 1265. Eles tiveram pelo menos 3 filhos e 2 filhas. Ele viveu em EDGEPELLICK ISLAND, Lower Alloways Creek Township, Salem, Nova Jérsei, Estados Unidos da América em 1266. Ele faleceu em 1306, em London, Inglaterra, com 80 anos, e foi sepultado em London, Inglaterra, United Kingdom.

ro.Brotherton Scotland: EDGEPELLICK ISLAND, Lower Alloways Creek Township, Salem, New Jersey, Statele Unite ale Americii

ru.Brotherton Scotland: эджепелликк исланд, лоуир аллоуеис крик тоуншип, сеилем, Нью-Джерси, Соединенные штаты
ru.lifeSummary: Sir Simon III Fraser, The Patriot Laird of Oliver Castle and Neidpath Castle родился в 1226 г. Он женился на женщине по имени Lady Mary Bisset of Oliver Castle перед 1265 г. У них было по крайней мере 3 сына и 2 дочери. Он жил в местности эджепелликк исланд, лоуир аллоуеис крик тоуншип, сеилем, Нью-Джерси, Соединенные штаты в 1266 году. Он умер в 1306 г. в местности Лондон, Англия в возрасте 80 лет и был похоронен в местности Лондон, Англия, Великобритания.

es.lifeSummary: Sir Simon III Fraser, The Patriot Laird of Oliver Castle and Neidpath Castle nació en 1226. Se casó con Lady Mary Bisset of Oliver Castle antes de 1265. Fueron padres de por lo menos 3 hijos y 2 hijas. Vivió en EDGEPELLICK ISLAND, Lower Alloways Creek Township, Salem, Nueva Jersey, Estados Unidos en 1266. Murió en 1306, en London, Inglaterra, a la edad de 80 años, y fue sepultado en London, Inglaterra, Reino Unido.
